items.evidence_text:
Client: I’m 54 and self-employed, and the market swings lately have me a little on edge. I want a plan that’s not… fragile.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain-English and practical. Going back to that volatility point—part of that is knowing what your baseline cash flow is. Roughly what’s your gross income in a year these days?
Client: It’s around 150k a year—maybe 155k? It varies a bit.
Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Client: I usually lump it all together, but honestly I’m not consistent.
Advisor: That’s normal. Let’s do it cleanly: total monthly expenses including everything—what’s the best estimate?
Client: If I’m being honest, it’s about $8,700 a month.
Advisor: Okay, $8,700 per month total expenses. Thanks. Now let’s isolate debt payments inside that—what’s your monthly debt cost, basically your mortgage payment?
Client: Mortgage is about $2,200 a month.

Client: Mortgage is about $2,200 a month.
Advisor: Okay—about $2,200. And that’s principal and interest, plus escrow, or just the loan payment?
Client: It’s the full payment with escrow. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. The payment I actually send is $2,300 a month.
Advisor: Got it—$2,300 monthly for the mortgage all-in. Thanks for correcting that.
Client: Yeah, I had the old number in my head.

Client: I thought it was like… nine hundred thousand? Maybe just under a million.
Advisor: Okay, around nine hundred to a million. If you had to pin it down—what’s the total you’re using right now?
Client: The total I’ve got written down is $941,100.
Advisor: Great—$941,100 investable assets. Let’s break that up a bit so we know what’s liquid versus not. Do you have a taxable brokerage account—non-retirement?
Client: Yes. That’s the biggest chunk—about $507,750.

Client: Correct—asset. Not debt.
Advisor: Good. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what do you estimate the home value at?
Client: About $373,000.
Advisor: $373,000 for the primary residence. Okay.
Client: And just to clarify—when you say “investable,” you’re not counting the house, right?

Client: I’m 54 and self-employed, and the market swings lately have me a little on edge. I want a plan that’s not… fragile.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain-English and practical. Going back to that volatility point—part of that is knowing what your baseline cash flow is. Roughly what’s your gross income in a year these days?

Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Lind. Going back to what you said earlier about the private markets piece matching the mortgage number, I’ve got those separated now.
Client: Yeah, good. That was making my brain itch.
Advisor: Totally get it. Let’s zoom out for a second and make sure the household basics are right. Lind, are you currently single, married, partnered—what should I put down?
Client: I’m single. Not married.
Advisor: Got it—single. And just to confirm household size: is it just you as an adult in the household, or anyone else living with you as an adult?

Client: Texas. TX.
Advisor: Great, Texas. And on taxes—do you have a sense of what bracket you usually land in? Not exact to the dollar, just the bracket.
Client: I think I’m in the 32% bracket most years.
Advisor: Okay—32%. When you say 32%, you mean your federal marginal bracket, not like your effective rate, right?
Client: Uh… I always mix that up. I mean the one my CPA says I’m “in.”

Client: Yes—one adult.
Advisor: Perfect. Next—what state are you a resident of for taxes? Like where you file from.
Client: Texas. TX.
Advisor: Great, Texas. And on taxes—do you have a sense of what bracket you usually land in? Not exact to the dollar, just the bracket.
Client: I think I’m in the 32% bracket most years.

Client: I’m 54 and self-employed, and the market swings lately have me a little on edge. I want a plan that’s not… fragile.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain-English and practical. Going back to that volatility point—part of that is knowing what your baseline cash flow is. Roughly what’s your gross income in a year these days?
Client: It’s around 150k a year—maybe 155k? It varies a bit.
Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Advisor: Hi Lind—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Thanks for making the time today.
Client: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks. I’m glad we’re doing this—been meaning to get it organized.
Advisor: Totally. Before we get into numbers, just at a high level—what made you reach out now?
Client: I’m 54 and self-employed, and the market swings lately have me a little on edge. I want a plan that’s not… fragile.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain-English and practical. Going back to that volatility point—part of that is knowing what your baseline cash flow is. Roughly what’s your gross income in a year these days?

Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier about being single in Texas—just to round out the household basics, Lind: are you based in the US overall? I know that sounds obvious, but I like to confirm it.
Client: Yeah, I’m in the US.
Advisor: Perfect. And the way my firm is set up, we operate as a US RIA—so that’s the market context I’m putting you in. Does that match what you expected, or were you thinking something else like a bank or insurance channel?
Client: Uh, I thought RIA was like… a broker-dealer? Sorry, I mix those up.
Advisor: Totally fair—people do. RIA is the advisory side; broker-dealer is more the transaction side. For our purposes, I’m just tagging this as US RIA. You’re good with that framing?

Advisor: Totally fair—people do. RIA is the advisory side; broker-dealer is more the transaction side. For our purposes, I’m just tagging this as US RIA. You’re good with that framing?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine.
Advisor: Great—one sec, I’m just noting that down. Now, risk tolerance: earlier you sounded pretty middle-of-the-road—want growth, but you don’t want big gut-punch drawdowns. Would you call yourself moderate?
Client: I’d say conservative, honestly.
Advisor: Okay—conservative. When you say conservative, do you mean like “I can’t handle seeing it down 10%,” or more like “I just don’t want anything wild,” but you can tolerate normal market swings?

Client: I’m 54 and self-employed, and the market swings lately have me a little on edge. I want a plan that’s not… fragile.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain-English and practical. Going back to that volatility point—part of that is knowing what your baseline cash flow is. Roughly what’s your gross income in a year these days?
Client: It’s around 150k a year—maybe 155k? It varies a bit.
Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Advisor: Okay, so about one-fifty to one-fifty-five. When you say gross, you mean before taxes and business expenses, right?
Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Client: I think it’s $154,650.
Advisor: Perfect—$154,650 gross annual. Thank you.
Advisor: Now, I’m going to ask a slightly weird question, Lind, just because it helps me bucket what you do. Would you say your day-to-day work is more operations—like running the business, logistics, making sure things happen—or more sales/marketing, or more technical?
Client: Operations, for sure. I’m basically the one keeping the trains running.
Advisor: Operations—okay. When you say “keeping the trains running,” is that like scheduling, vendors, fulfillment, that kind of stuff?

Advisor: Good. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what do you estimate the home value at?
Client: About $373,000.
Advisor: $373,000 for the primary residence. Okay.
Client: And just to clarify—when you say “investable,” you’re not counting the house, right?
Advisor: Exactly—investable excludes the home value. The home is still part of your net worth, just not typically something we draw from easily.

Client: Correct, I’m the primary.
Advisor: Great. One more housekeeping item, and then we’ll get back to the accounts—what’s your date of birth, Lind?
Client: August 20th, 1972.
Advisor: August 20, 1972—thank you.
Advisor: And sorry—when you said “August 20th,” I just want to make sure I didn’t mis-hear the year. You said 1972, not 1973?

Client: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks. I’m glad we’re doing this—been meaning to get it organized.
Advisor: Totally. Before we get into numbers, just at a high level—what made you reach out now?

Client: It’s around 150k a year—maybe 155k? It varies a bit.

Client: Uh—wait, I mean what I pay myself. So… is that gross or net?

Advisor: Perfect—$154,650 gross annually. I’m writing that down. And is it just you in the household—one adult?
Client: Yes, just me. One adult.
Advisor: Great. Now, on spending—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Not just bills, but food, insurance, travel, the whole thing.
Client: I think like… eight thousand a month? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Okay—eight-ish. When you say “expenses,” are you including your mortgage payment in that, or is that separate in your head?

Advisor: Yeah, it’s been weird—sunny and then, like, sideways rain. Okay, Lind, going back to the balance sheet stuff you mentioned earlier—before we move on, I want to make sure I’m labeling you correctly in my notes.
Client: Sure.
Advisor: So in my system I have you tagged as “Client 1.” Is that right—you're Client 1 on this plan?
Client: Uh—no, I think I’m Client 0. It’s just me, so I’m the first one.
Advisor: Oh—good catch. When you say “Client 0,” you mean you’re the only person, so you’re the first slot, basically?

Advisor: Quick tangent—are you staying local today or did you have to fight traffic to get here?
Client: Oh, traffic was awful. I almost rescheduled.
Advisor: I hear you. Okay—back to it. On income, you said self-employed and you pay yourself—do you also have Social Security coming in yet, or not at this point?
Client: No, not yet. That’s later.
Advisor: Got it—so right now it’s your self-employment income driving that $154,650 annual gross.

Client: Correct—asset. Not debt.
Advisor: Good. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what do you estimate the home value at?
Client: About $373,000.
Advisor: $373,000 for the primary residence. Okay.
Client: And just to clarify—when you say “investable,” you’re not counting the house, right?

Advisor: Yeah, it’s been weird—sunny and then, like, sideways rain. Okay, Lind, going back to the balance sheet stuff you mentioned earlier—before we move on, I want to make sure I’m labeling you correctly in my notes.
Client: Sure.
Advisor: So in my system I have you tagged as “Client 1.” Is that right—you're Client 1 on this plan?
Client: Uh—no, I think I’m Client 0. It’s just me, so I’m the first one.
Advisor: Oh—good catch. When you say “Client 0,” you mean you’re the only person, so you’re the first slot, basically?

Client: No, you didn’t ask it earlier. You’re good.
Advisor: Okay, good—sometimes these calls blur together.
Advisor: While I’ve got you, Lind—going back to what we covered earlier on the balance sheet: we talked through your taxable brokerage, your 401k/IRA bucket, the bank-ish retirement platform account, and that private markets piece, plus the house and the mortgage. Still all accurate in your mind?
Client: Yeah, that all sounds right.
Advisor: And just to clear up one small thing—earlier you mentioned Social Security in passing, and I might’ve mentally filed it under “income.” But your $154,650 is business gross income, not Social Security, right?

Advisor: Good. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what do you estimate the home value at?
Client: About $373,000.
Advisor: $373,000 for the primary residence. Okay.
Client: And just to clarify—when you say “investable,” you’re not counting the house, right?
Advisor: Exactly—investable excludes the home value. The home is still part of your net worth, just not typically something we draw from easily.

Advisor: Okay—good. And sorry, quick micro-clarifier: when you say “mine,” there isn’t a business entity on it, it’s personally owned by you?
Client: Correct, personally. Not the business.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to the home for one second—just so I label it consistently—your house is an asset type of property, specifically your primary residence. Fair?
Client: Yeah, it’s property—my house.
Advisor: Cool. And then looping back to the bigger picture—so we’ve talked income, assets, and the mortgage. We still don’t have any life or disability policies in place, right?

Client: The total I’ve got written down is $941,100.
Advisor: Great—$941,100 investable assets. Let’s break that up a bit so we know what’s liquid versus not. Do you have a taxable brokerage account—non-retirement?
Client: Yes. That’s the biggest chunk—about $507,750.
Advisor: $507,750 in taxable brokerage. And then retirement accounts—401(k) or IRA type—what’s that bucket?
Client: Around $209,100.

Advisor: $154,700 outstanding. And when does that mortgage finish—around 2032?
Client: Yeah, April 2032 is the final payment, I believe.
Advisor: Perfect. Okay—switching to assets. Going back to your “not fragile” comment, the mix matters. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so brokerage, retirement accounts, cash-like accounts, private investments—excluding your home?
Client: I thought it was like… nine hundred thousand? Maybe just under a million.
Advisor: Okay, around nine hundred to a million. If you had to pin it down—what’s the total you’re using right now?
